entry.title: An Open Achievement Standard for On-Chain Games
entry.summary: Steam has achievements. Xbox has Gamerscore. PlayStation has Trophies. But on-chain games have no standard way to track player accomplishments across games. We built one — an open standard that any EffectStream game can implement, plus a portal where players can see all their achievements in one place.
entry.content:
Steam has achievements. Xbox has Gamerscore. PlayStation has Trophies. But on-chain games have no standard way to track player accomplishments across games. We built one — an open standard that any EffectStream game can implement, plus a portal where players can see all their achievements in one place.

Defining the Standard​

Every platform-locked achievement system (Steam, Xbox, PlayStation) shares the same limitation: your gaming history is tied to your account on that platform. If you switch, you start from zero. On-chain games have the opportunity to do better — achievements tied to a wallet are portable by default.

We created PRC-1 (Paima Request for Comment), a community standard similar to CIPs for Cardano or ERCs for Ethereum. PRC-1 defines how games register, track, and expose achievements via a standard API. Any EffectStream node that implements PRC-1 exposes two HTTP endpoints:

  • GET /achievements/public/list — returns all possible achievements in a game, their descriptions, and global completion statistics
  • GET /achievements/wallet/:walletAddress — returns a specific player's progress, including which achievements they've unlocked, completion dates, and progress toward incomplete ones

The standard was later extended into PRC-6, which adds metric channels and leaderboard rankings for integration with the broader Midnight Platform. PRC-6 defines a richer set of endpoints (/metrics, /metrics/{channel}, /metrics/users/{address}) that allow the platform to aggregate data across all participating games.

  • PRC-1 specification
  • PRC-6 specification and reference implementation
  • Achievement API documentation

Proof of Concept — Integrating Multiple Games​

To validate the standard, we integrated it into multiple live games. The achievement system is now running in:

  • Dust-2-Dust — ZK deck-building dungeon crawler
  • Block Kart Legends — cross-chain EVM + ZK racing
  • Safe Solver — L2 risk-reward game on Midnight
  • Kachina Kolosseum — ZK commit-reveal PvP arena
  • Tower Defense — open-source tower defense template

Integrating achievements into a game is straightforward. You define your achievements as metadata in your API entry point, and EffectStream handles the rest — exposing PRC-1 compliant endpoints automatically:

const achievementList: Achievement[] = [
    {
        name: "finish-chapter-1",
        displayName: "Over The River",
        description: "Finish Chapter 1.",
        isActive: true,
    },
    {
        name: "win-10-battles",
        displayName: "Battle Hardened",
        description: "Win 10 battles against any opponent.",
        isActive: true,
    },
];

const metadata: AchievementMetadata = {
    game: { id: "my-awesome-game" },
    list: achievementList,
};

export const achievements = Promise.resolve(metadata);

Tracking progress happens inside your State Transition Functions. When a player performs an action that matters for an achievement, you read their current progress and update it:

import { getAchievementProgress, setAchievementProgress } from '@effectstream/db';

async function handleBattleWin(walletId: number, blockTimestamp: Date, dbConn: Pool) {
  const current = await getAchievementProgress.run(
    { wallet: walletId, names: ['win-10-battles'] }, dbConn
  );
  const row = current[0];
  if (!row?.completed_date) {
    const newProgress = (row?.progress ?? 0) + 1;
    return [[setAchievementProgress, {
      name: 'win-10-battles',
      wallet: walletId,
      completed_date: newProgress >= 10 ? blockTimestamp : null,
      progress: newProgress,
      total: 10,
    }]];
  }
  return [];
}

You can see a real-world example in the Tower Defense backend, where achievements are registered in a few lines at the game's entry point.

Designing the Achievement Portal​

With every game exposing the same API, we could build a portal that aggregates achievements from all of them. Players connect their wallet and see all their accomplishments in one place — across every EffectStream game they've played.

The portal doesn't use a centralized database. It queries each game's PRC-1 endpoints at runtime and presents a unified view. This means any new game that implements the standard automatically appears in the portal — no integration work required on the portal side.

We designed the portal UI in Figma, focusing on a clean presentation that surfaces progress across games, completion percentages, and unlocked achievements with timestamps.

  • Design (Figma)

TODO: SCREENSHOTS — portal design showing the achievement list, per-game breakdown, and progress indicators

Building and Launching the Portal​

The achievement portal is live at midnight.fun/games, where players can browse achievements across all integrated games. The portal implementation is open-source:

  • Portal source code
  • Announcement

TODO: SCREENSHOTS — live portal at midnight.fun showing achievements from multiple games

The portal connects to the PRC-1 endpoints of each registered game and aggregates the data into a single player profile. When you connect your wallet, you see every achievement you've earned across Dust-2-Dust, Block Kart Legends, Safe Solver, Kachina Kolosseum, and any future game that implements the standard.

The Achievement Ecosystem​

The achievement system is now live across 5+ games, with the achievement code built directly into EffectStream. Any new game built with the framework gets achievement support out of the box — define your achievements, track progress in your STFs, and the API is served automatically.

Unlike platform-locked achievements, these are on-chain and portable. Your gaming history follows your wallet, not your platform account. And because the standard is open, anyone can build their own portal, integrate achievement data into their application, or create cross-game experiences that reward players for their accomplishments across the entire ecosystem.

TODO: VIDEO — walkthrough of the complete achievement ecosystem: multiple games, one portal, one player profile

entry.title: Auto-Sign: Eliminating Wallet Pop-ups for Real-Time Applications
entry.summary: WRITE: Every blockchain interaction that requires a wallet signature creates friction. In a turn-based game, you might tolerate one pop-up per move. In a real-time game, you need dozens of signatures per second. Auto-sign solves this by delegating non-financial signing to a session key — your wallet approves once, then the app signs on your behalf.
entry.content:
WRITE: Every blockchain interaction that requires a wallet signature creates friction. In a turn-based game, you might tolerate one pop-up per move. In a real-time game, you need dozens of signatures per second. Auto-sign solves this by delegating non-financial signing to a session key — your wallet approves once, then the app signs on your behalf.

Designing the Specification​

We started by writing a formal specification for the auto-sign approach. The key insight is separating two types of blockchain interactions:

  • Financial transactions (asset transfers, contract calls with value) — these always require explicit wallet approval
  • Non-financial data submissions (game moves, messages, state updates) — these can be safely delegated to a session key

ADD LINK to specification document if public

WRITE: Explain the security model — what guarantees does the user still have? The session key is scoped (can only submit data, not move funds), time-bounded (expires after a session), and revocable (user can cancel delegation at any time).

Cardano Wallet Implementation​

The first implementation was in a Cardano wallet — proof that the specification works in practice with a real wallet and real transactions.

ADD: Which wallet was used? Link to the code and/or demo

WRITE: Any Cardano-specific challenges? The UTXO model handles signing differently from the account model — did this require adjustments to the spec?

MetaMask Snap for EVM Chains​

We then ported auto-sign to EVM chains via a MetaMask Snap. Snaps are plugins for MetaMask that extend its functionality — in this case, adding session key delegation.

WRITE: Brief explanation of MetaMask Snaps for readers who haven't encountered them — they're sandboxed JavaScript modules that run inside MetaMask, letting developers add custom signing logic.

This means auto-sign now works cross-chain: Cardano users get it through the native wallet integration, and EVM users get it through the MetaMask Snap.

ADD: Link to Snap code, install instructions

ADD VIDEO HERE demonstrating the snap — show the contrast between the normal flow (pop-up on every action) and the auto-sign flow (approve once, play freely)

Live in a Game​

To prove this works in a real application, we integrated auto-sign into Tower Defense — a real-time strategy game where players place and upgrade towers while waves of enemies approach.

  • Game source code
  • Play the game

FIX: Game is currently not working. Must fix before publishing this article

WRITE: Before/after comparison — what the gameplay felt like with manual signing (constant interruptions, broken flow) vs. auto-sign (seamless, feels like a normal game). How many signatures does a typical game session generate?

ADD VIDEO HERE showing gameplay — focus on the seamless experience with no pop-ups

Conclusion​

WRITE: Impact summary — auto-sign transforms what's possible for real-time blockchain applications. It's not just games — chat apps, IoT dashboards, collaborative tools, anything that needs rapid interaction benefits from session key delegation. The model is secure (limited scope, time-bounded, revocable) and cross-chain (Cardano + EVM today, extensible to other chains).

ADD VIDEO HERE: Close-out demo showing the end-to-end flow — wallet approval, delegation, gameplay, session end

entry.title: Data Availability with Avail: Building High-Throughput Applications
entry.summary: WRITE putting everything on the settlement layer is slow and expensive. Data Availability (DA) layers like Avail let you post data cheaply while keeping settlement on Cardano or EVM. EffectStream now supports Avail as a first-class data source.
entry.content:
WRITE: On-chain apps that handle lots of data (games with frequent moves, social feeds, IoT data) hit a wall: putting everything on the settlement layer is slow and expensive. Data Availability (DA) layers like Avail let you post data cheaply while keeping settlement on Cardano or EVM. EffectStream now supports Avail as a first-class data source.

Reading Avail Blockchain State​

We added an Avail funnel to EffectStream that runs in parallel with other chain funnels. Avail blocks arrive independently from the settlement chain, so the funnel ingests them concurrently — no blocking, no waiting for one chain to catch up to another.

  • Parallel funnel implementation
  • Documentation

WRITE: Why a parallel funnel matters — in a multi-chain app, you don't want your settlement chain to bottleneck your data layer. Explain how the parallel architecture keeps both chains processing at their native speed.

Avail Wallet and Batcher Support​

Users can now submit data via Avail natively. We added Avail wallet support to the batcher system, which aggregates user transactions and posts them to the Avail DA layer.

  • Implementation PR

WRITE: What the batcher does for Avail — aggregates individual user submissions into batches, posts them to Avail for data availability, while settlement happens on the main chain. This gives you Avail's throughput with your settlement chain's security.

Hybrid dApps — Choosing Your Data Layer​

With Avail support, developers can build hybrid applications where different data goes to different layers:

  • High-throughput data (game moves, chat messages, sensor readings) → Avail DA layer
  • Settlement data (asset transfers, contract state changes) → Cardano or EVM

EffectStream's state machine reacts to events from all configured chains seamlessly. The developer decides where each type of data lives, and the framework handles the rest.

WRITE: Architecture explanation or diagram — how a developer configures which data goes where. What does the config look like? How does the state machine receive events from both layers?

When to Use a Data Availability Layer​

WRITE: Decision guide for developers — when does a DA layer make sense?

Topics to cover:

  • Avail Turbo and high-throughput scenarios — when your app generates more data than the settlement layer can handle
  • Lessons from Tarochi — we built Tarochi with EVM as the DA layer, and ran into sync time issues. What we learned about choosing the right DA layer.
  • Cardano limitations — no event standard like Ethereum, heavy indexing requirements. How DA layers can complement Cardano's strengths.

IMPLEMENT: Add this as a docs page and reference from this blog post

Conclusion​

To bring it all together, we built a game template that combines Cardano and Avail state into a single application — settlement on Cardano, high-frequency data on Avail.

  • Template code

ACTION: Merge this PR or verify it's up to date

WRITE: Summarize — EffectStream makes DA layers a first-class option. Developers choose where their data lives without changing their application architecture. The same state machine processes events from both layers.

ADD VIDEO HERE showing the Cardano + Avail template in action

entry.title: Engine Integrations: From GameMaker to Mobile AR
entry.summary: EffectStream is designed to integrate with any game engine or platform. This article covers five distinct integrations — each demonstrating a different technology combination, from traditional game engines to social platform frames to mobile augmented reality.
entry.content:
EffectStream is designed to integrate with any game engine or platform. This article covers five distinct integrations — each demonstrating a different technology combination, from traditional game engines to social platform frames to mobile augmented reality.

GameMaker Integration​

Game engines like Unreal and Unity dominate game development, but they aren't always the right fit. GameMaker is optimized for 2D games, powers titles like Undertale and Hotline Miami, and is free for non-commercial use. The catch: GameMaker uses its own language (GML), not JavaScript — which has historically made web3 integration difficult.

We built a custom adapter that bridges GML to EffectStream's JavaScript middleware. GameMaker developers can now access wallet connections and on-chain state without writing any JavaScript directly. The middleware is exposed via a GameMaker extension with a single PaimaMW function — keeping the integration surface minimal while unlocking the full power of EffectStream's state machine framework.

Because GameMaker supports HTML5 export, the resulting games run directly in the browser — no downloads, no plugins.

You can also watch the full walkthrough on YouTube.

  • Template code
  • Frontend documentation
  • Full write-up

Modern Local Development with Hardhat and Dolos​

One of the biggest friction points for multi-chain developers is setting up a local development environment. Syncing a Cardano indexer like Carp can take days on testnet and even longer on mainnet — making rapid iteration nearly impossible.

We integrated Hardhat v3 for EVM contract development and Dolos with UTxO-RPC for Cardano indexing. Together, they replace the heavy Carp sync with an instant localhost setup.

The old workflow looked like this: install Carp, wait hours or days for it to sync the Cardano chain, configure a separate EVM node, then wire everything together. With the new setup, a single command spins up both an EVM node (via Hardhat) and a Cardano node (via Dolos) with UTxO-RPC endpoints ready to go. EffectStream connects to both through its unified sync protocol, and you're writing and testing multi-chain game logic in minutes instead of days.

This is particularly impactful for onboarding new developers — the barrier to entry drops from "spend a weekend setting up infrastructure" to "run one command."

TODO: VIDEO — Demo of spinning up local EVM + Cardano dev environment

TODO: DOCUMENTATION — Step-by-step guide for setting up the local multi-chain dev environment

  • PR: Local development nodes

Onchain Payments with Transak​

Most blockchain games require users to already own cryptocurrency before they can participate. This is a massive onboarding barrier — mainstream gamers don't have wallets or tokens ready to go.

We built a template that integrates Transak to allow fiat-to-crypto purchases of in-game items directly within the game interface. Players can buy items with a credit card, and the purchase flows through an EVM smart contract that EffectStream monitors. The game state machine processes the purchase event and updates the player's inventory on-chain.

The architecture follows a clean separation: a React frontend displays the item shop, Transak handles the payment flow and KYC, the EVM contract records ownership, and EffectStream's state machine keeps the game world consistent. Developers using this template get a working end-to-end payment pipeline out of the box.

  • Template code

Farcaster Frame Integration​

Farcaster is a decentralized social network where posts can embed interactive mini-applications called Frames. When a user shares a link, Farcaster looks for special <meta> tags in the page. If it finds them, it renders buttons directly below the post — these buttons can be links, or they can trigger on-chain actions like signing an EVM transaction on one of Farcaster's approved networks.

We built a template that turns an EffectStream game into a Farcaster Frame — playable without leaving the social feed, no app install required. The template implements a collaborative painting game on Arbitrum:

  1. Find a posted canvas on Farcaster that isn't full yet
  2. Choose a color and paint — you can contribute as many times as you like
  3. After contributing, fork the canvas to your own timeline
  4. New paints to your fork reward you, and you can withdraw earnings by visiting your post

EffectStream monitors the Arbitrum contract for paint and fork events, updates the canvas state, and serves the updated Frame image at the same URL. Every share becomes a playable instance — distribution through social feeds is a fundamentally different growth model from "download our app."

The Frame uses a secondary EVM contract as an EffectStream Primitive because Farcaster only supports explicit transactions on whitelisted networks, not arbitrary wallet signing.

Farcaster has since introduced Frames v2 (Mini Apps), which expand on the same principle with richer interactivity — the template demonstrates the core pattern that both versions build on.

  • Template code
  • Farcaster Frame template PR

Mobile: Android with GPS​

The most ambitious integration: a native Android application combining GPS location data with on-chain game logic through EffectStream.

The app is built in Kotlin and uses WalletConnect to connect mobile wallets. Players' real-world GPS coordinates are posted as on-chain events through EffectStream's batcher, and the game state machine processes location data to drive gameplay — location-based encounters, territory claiming, or proximity-based interactions.

The template includes a full Paima middleware bridge for Android (PaimaBridge.kt), handling the JavaScript-to-native communication that lets the Android app interact with EffectStream's middleware layer. The architecture proves that EffectStream works beyond the browser — the same state machine framework that powers web games can drive native mobile experiences.

TODO: AR feature — 3D asset selection overlaid on camera feed

TODO: Deploy to Google Play and gather user feedback

  • Template code

Conclusion​

From browser-based 2D games (GameMaker) to instant local dev environments (Hardhat + Dolos) to fiat payment rails (Transak) to social feed distribution (Farcaster Frames) to native mobile apps (Android GPS) — each integration shows a different facet of EffectStream's flexibility. The state machine framework adapts to wherever users are, and every template is open-source for anyone to build on.

entry.title: Game Template Library: Five On-Chain Game Patterns
entry.summary: The best way to learn a framework is to see it in action. We built five distinct game templates — each showcasing a different chain integration, cryptographic technique, or architectural pattern. All are open-source and playable live. This article walks through each one and explains the technology behind it.
entry.content:
The best way to learn a framework is to see it in action. We built five distinct game templates — each showcasing a different chain integration, cryptographic technique, or architectural pattern. All are open-source and playable live. This article walks through each one and explains the technology behind it.

Trading Card Game — Cryptographic Card Mechanics​

How do you deal cards fairly on a public blockchain? Every transaction is visible, so a naive implementation would let opponents see your hand. We solved this with a novel cryptographic protocol for hidden-hand card games.

The core problem is that there's no trusted dealer in a decentralized game. Both players need to agree on a shuffled deck without either side being able to peek. Our approach uses a commit-reveal cryptographic scheme where the deck state is committed as hashes, and card reveals are verified against those commitments. The protocol guarantees that neither player can manipulate the shuffle or see cards they shouldn't — the same fairness properties as a physical deck, but enforced by math instead of trust.

The template implements a full trading card game with deck building, hand management, and turn-based combat — all running on-chain through EffectStream's state machine.

  • Template code
  • Cryptography design document
  • Documentation

Safe Solver — EffectStream as an L2 on Midnight​

EffectStream provides a TypeScript L2 runtime that merges blocks from multiple chains — EVM and Midnight ZK — into a single unified stream for executing game logic. Safe Solver is the template that demonstrates this architecture in a playable game.

The game itself is a risk-reward safe-cracking challenge built with Three.js. Each level presents 3–7 safes, with only one hiding an alarm. Players drill safes to earn gold, with escalating risk: fewer safes mean higher base points but worse odds. Bank your points with "KEEP REWARD" or push your luck — trigger the alarm and lose everything unbanked. Scores are saved to a leaderboard on the Midnight blockchain.

What makes this architecturally interesting is how EffectStream acts as the execution layer. The game's TypeScript state machine processes turns, computes outcomes, and manages player state, while Midnight provides the privacy-preserving settlement layer and EVM handles token contracts. EffectStream merges both chains' blocks into a single stream, so the game logic sees one coherent timeline regardless of which chain originated the event.

  • Source code
  • Play live

Kachina Kolosseum — ZK Commit-Reveal PvP​

In a PvP game on a public blockchain, the second player has an unfair advantage: they can see the first player's move on-chain and counter it. ZK commit-reveal eliminates this problem.

Kachina Kolosseum is a turn-based arena battler where two players face off with teams of 3 heroes. Each hero has equipment loadouts (axes, shields, bows, swords, spears) and stances (Defensive, Neutral, Aggressive) that multiply damage. The combat flow works like this:

  1. Player 1 commits — selects attack targets and stances, then submits a zero-knowledge hash of those choices. The moves are hidden on-chain.
  2. Player 2 submits — moves openly, since Player 1's commitment is already locked.
  3. Player 1 reveals — the ZK circuit verifies the revealed moves match the original hash, then resolves damage for both sides simultaneously.

This prevents front-running or move-snooping. If Player 1 tries to reveal different moves than they committed, the ZK proof fails and the transaction is rejected. If a player refuses to reveal, the game can resolve with a timeout penalty.

  • Play live
  • Source code

Block Kart Legends — Cross-Chain EVM + ZK Racing​

Block Kart Legends demonstrates how EffectStream's L2 can combine EVM and Midnight in parallel to produce complex game outcomes. The engine merges results from both chains into a virtual execution layer where heavyweight computations — like simulating an entire race with thousands of steps and variables — can take place in TypeScript while both chains provide the trust guarantees.

Players allocate 25 stat points across Speed, Acceleration, Weight, Handling, and Traction, then select items for strategic use during the race. The backend simulates the full race using on-chain data, while the Three.js frontend runs a parallel simulation for immediate visual feedback. The ZK layer on Midnight ensures race configuration integrity, and the EVM layer on Arbitrum handles the token and reward contracts.

This template proves that EffectStream's multi-chain merge architecture isn't just for turn-based games. Real-time simulations with complex physics and thousands of state transitions can run on the L2, anchored to both a privacy-preserving ZK chain and a high-throughput EVM chain.

  • Play live
  • Source code

Dust-2-Dust — Pushing the Limits of ZK Contracts​

Dust-2-Dust is a fully on-chain deck-building dungeon crawler where all game logic is secured by zero-knowledge proofs on the Midnight network. It's our most technically ambitious template — not because of the game design, but because of what we had to solve to make ZK contracts handle this level of complexity.

ZK circuits are fundamentally constrained. Even simple operations are expensive to prove, and Midnight's Compact language compiles down to circuits where every branch and lookup costs real proof-generation time. A combat round in Dust-2-Dust involves: resolving 3 player abilities (each with primary effects, energy-triggered secondary effects, and elemental types), computing damage against up to 3 enemies (each with physical/fire/ice resistances, blocking, healing, and ally buffs), rotating a 7-card deck with non-repeating draws, and updating all state — in a single ZK circuit.

We developed novel techniques to make this work efficiently in Compact:

  • Witness-validated division: ZK circuits don't natively support division, so we use an external witness that provides the quotient and remainder, then validate x == y * quotient + remainder inside the circuit.
  • Boolean arithmetic for conditional math: to avoid underflow checks in non-executed branches, all conditional damage calculations use multiplication by boolean flags ((condition as Uint<1>) * value) instead of if/else.
  • Dynamic indexing via circuit unrolling: ZK circuits only support constant indices, but deck rotation requires dynamic access. We unroll lookups into exhaustive if-chains over all possible indices.
  • Deterministic per-player RNG: each player has a persistent hash chain (persistentHash of the previous seed), ensuring reproducible randomness without a trusted oracle.

The game features biome-based quest progression, boss battles with increasing difficulty gates, an ability upgrade system (sacrifice + gold cost), and an energy-color synergy system where abilities trigger bonus effects based on teammates' energy generation. The entire 970-line Compact contract runs in ZK — every combat round, every ability upgrade, every quest finalization produces a zero-knowledge proof.

  • Play live
  • Source code

Five templates, five different approaches to on-chain gaming:

  • Trading Cards — cryptographic card mechanics without a trusted dealer
  • Safe Solver — EffectStream as an L2 merging EVM + Midnight into one stream
  • Kachina Kolosseum — ZK commit-reveal for fair PvP combat
  • Block Kart Legends — cross-chain EVM + ZK racing with complex simulation
  • Dust-2-Dust — a full dungeon crawler pushing ZK contract complexity to its limits

These aren't just demos. Each template is a complete, deployable game that developers can fork, modify, and ship. The source code is open and the live versions are playable today.

entry.title: Cross-Chain Privacy: Mina Integration and Zero-Knowledge Proofs
entry.summary: WRITE: Cardano provides strong settlement guarantees but all state is public. Mina's ZK-native design enables private state. By integrating both chains, EffectStream apps can have public settlement on Cardano with private computation via Mina — and later, local ZK proofs that don't require any ZK chain at all.
entry.content:
WRITE: Cardano provides strong settlement guarantees but all state is public. Mina's ZK-native design enables private state. By integrating both chains, EffectStream apps can have public settlement on Cardano with private computation via Mina — and later, local ZK proofs that don't require any ZK chain at all.

Reading Mina Blockchain State​

EffectStream uses a "funnel" abstraction to read data from multiple blockchains through a unified interface. We added a Mina funnel, enabling the framework to parse and index Mina blockchain state alongside Cardano and EVM chains.

WRITE: Brief explanation of the funnel system — what it abstracts, how adding a new chain works. A developer's state machine receives events from all configured chains without needing to know the details of each chain's data format.

ADD: Link to Mina funnel docs and code

Mina Wallet Support​

Beyond reading Mina state, we added full Mina wallet support to EffectStream and the batcher system. Users can sign transactions with Mina wallets, making Mina a first-class chain in the framework.

WRITE: What the batcher is (briefly, for readers new to EffectStream) — it aggregates user transactions and posts them to the blockchain, reducing per-user costs and enabling gasless interactions.

Building a Cross-Chain Game with Private State​

To demonstrate the power of combining Cardano and Mina, we built a game template that uses both chains in a single application. Mina adds private state that wouldn't be possible natively on Cardano.

  • Source code

WRITE: Concrete example — what private information does the game use? Hidden cards? Secret positions? A fog-of-war mechanic? How does Mina's ZK capability change what's possible in gameplay compared to a Cardano-only game?

ADD VIDEO HERE showing the game in action — highlight the moment where private state matters

Local Zero-Knowledge Proof Generation​

After integrating with Mina's chain, we took the next step: computing ZK proofs locally without needing to settle to any ZK chain at all. This removes the dependency on Mina's network while retaining the privacy guarantees.

  • Same template codebase
  • Recursive proof research

We're also working on recursive proof support — not just single proofs, but proofs that can verify other proofs, enabling more complex privacy-preserving computations.

WRITE: Technical explanation — local ZK vs. chain-based ZK. What are the trade-offs?

  • Latency: local proofs are faster (no network round-trip)
  • Cost: no on-chain fees for proof verification
  • Trust: what trust assumptions change when you move from chain-verified to locally-verified proofs?

WRITE: What recursive proofs enable that single proofs don't — composable privacy, incremental state verification, etc.

Conclusion​

WRITE: Summarize the journey — from reading Mina state, to wallet support, to a full cross-chain game, to removing the chain dependency entirely with local proofs. Each step expanded what's possible for privacy-preserving applications.

  • Full template code

ADD: README instructions for running the template locally, frontend demo link

ADD VIDEO HERE showing the complete privacy-enabled game

entry.title: Building a Cross-Chain NFT Launchpad
entry.summary: WRITE: NFT launches are complex — creators need smart contracts for the sale, a UI for buyers, multi-chain payment support, proof of participation, and marketplace integration. We built a full launchpad platform that handles all of this, with EffectStream powering the backend state management.
entry.content:
WRITE: NFT launches are complex — creators need smart contracts for the sale, a UI for buyers, multi-chain payment support, proof of participation, and marketplace integration. We built a full launchpad platform that handles all of this, with EffectStream powering the backend state management.

The Pre-Order Smart Contract​

The foundation is a smart contract that handles the NFT pre-order lifecycle: deposits, campaign management, and refund mechanics.

ADD: Link to contract code

WRITE: How the contract works — what happens when a user deposits? How does a creator start and end a campaign? What are the refund conditions?

The Launchpad Backend​

The backend is powered by EffectStream, with each presale running as its own deployment.

  • Backend code
  • Portal integration

WRITE: Why separate deployments? If all campaigns shared one EffectStream instance, you'd need to resync one very long chain of events for every new campaign — especially painful if campaigns monitor multiple chains for payments. Separate deployments keep sync times fast and independent.

WRITE: How EffectStream's NTP-based sync (instead of block-based) makes this architecture more efficient — the "main clock" is an NTP server (very cheap) rather than a blockchain, so you're not wasting RPC calls fetching blocks you don't care about.

DECISION: Should we try to launch this service publicly?

ADD: GIF showing core flows — create campaign, contribute, view status

The Launchpad UI​

A full user interface for managing campaigns — creators can set up sales, track progress, and manage payouts. Buyers can browse campaigns, contribute funds, and track their participation.

  • Launchpad portal components

ADD VIDEO HERE: Walk-through of all dApp functionality — create a campaign, contribute as a buyer, see the dashboard

ADD: Screenshots or Figma link showing the UI design

Cross-Chain Payments and Proof of Ownership​

The launchpad supports participation from both Cardano and EVM wallets. Users can contribute from whichever chain they prefer.

We also built proof-of-ownership endpoints so participants can verify their contribution to a sale:

  • Ownership verification API

WRITE: Explain the cross-chain evolution — we originally planned to use bridge payments (Milkomeda for Cardano↔EVM), but Milkomeda became non-viable and alternatives like Squid were still experimental. We pivoted to native chain primitives — accepting payments directly on each chain. This turned out to be simpler, more reliable, and didn't depend on third-party bridge infrastructure.

We use Aiken smart contracts for transaction metadata validation and merkle trees to efficiently prove participation.

BUILD: UI for proving sale participation (~1 day of work)

Conclusion​

The end-to-end cross-chain flow: a user participates in a sale from their Cardano wallet, receives an NFT, transfers it to EVM, and lists it on OpenSea — all managed by a single platform.

WRITE: Summarize what the launchpad enables — creators get a turnkey platform for multi-chain NFT sales, buyers get a familiar experience regardless of which chain they use.

INVESTIGATE: What exactly was the marketplace integration? Which marketplace, what was the integration flow?

ADD VIDEO HERE showing the full flow

entry.title: Projected NFTs: Using Cardano NFTs in Smart Contract Applications
entry.summary: WRITE: What problem does Projected NFT solve? Users want to use their NFTs in apps (games, DeFi) without transferring ownership. Projected NFTs let you "lock" an NFT on Cardano L1 and use a representation in your application — the NFT never leaves your wallet in a meaningful sense.
entry.content:
WRITE: What problem does Projected NFT solve? Users want to use their NFTs in apps (games, DeFi) without transferring ownership. Projected NFTs let you "lock" an NFT on Cardano L1 and use a representation in your application — the NFT never leaves your wallet in a meaningful sense.

The Aiken Smart Contract​

WRITE: Explain the Projected NFT concept in detail: lock an NFT on Cardano L1, use it in an app without transferring ownership.

The smart contract is written in Aiken, Cardano's Rust-based smart contract language. A CI pipeline validates that the contract compiles successfully on every change.

ADD: Link to contract code in paima-engine repo

A JavaScript Library for NFT Projection​

To make it easy for any developer to project and unproject NFTs programmatically, we built a JavaScript library that wraps the smart contract interaction.

WRITE: Brief code snippet or API overview showing how simple it is to use — e.g. projectNFT(policyId, assetName) style

The library works in both browser and Node.js environments.

Indexing NFT State​

To track NFT lock/unlock events in real-time, we built a Carp task — Carp is a Cardano blockchain indexer that lets applications query on-chain state efficiently.

The indexer comes with an OpenAPI definition, so any application can query projection status through a standard REST API.

WRITE: Expand on what the Carp task tracks — lock events, unlock events, current projection status per NFT

The Projection dApp​

We built a full dApp for projecting and managing NFTs with a visual interface.

  • Source code
  • Hosted dApp
  • Demo on Twitter

FIX: dApp is currently not working. Need to fix and verify before publishing this article

ADD VIDEO HERE — existing video: https://drive.google.com/file/d/12HzmHV7HI8msoc1yvI6zRqsZL69TSBeA/view

WRITE: Walk through the user flow — connect wallet, select NFT, project it, see it in-app

Framework Integration​

The indexer is connected to EffectStream's funnel system — the abstraction layer that reads data from multiple blockchains. This means any EffectStream application can react to NFT projection events directly in its state machine.

  • Documentation
  • Implementation PR
  • Example dApp

WRITE: Explain the integration architecture — how does an EffectStream app receive and process projection events? What does the state machine handler look like?

Conclusion and What's Next​

WRITE: Summarize the full stack — contract, library, indexer, dApp, framework integration. Five layers that together let any Cardano NFT have utility inside an application without leaving the user's wallet.

WRITE: What this enables — games where your Cardano NFTs have in-game powers, DeFi where you can collateralize without transferring, etc.

ADD VIDEO HERE: Close-out video showing the full end-to-end flow

WRITE: Mention challenges — Cardano testing requires heavy node sync (Carp can take days on testnet/mainnet), and no Cardano wallet currently supports localhost connections (unlike MetaMask for EVM). These are ecosystem-wide issues that tools like yaci-devkit and Dolos are starting to address.

entry.title: Stake Pool Delegation: Connecting Cardano SPOs to On-Chain Applications
entry.summary: WRITE: Cardano's stake pool ecosystem has millions of ADA delegated across hundreds of pools, but delegation has been a one-way relationship — delegators earn rewards, and that's it. What if games and apps could react to where you delegate? What if stake pool operators could offer in-game benefits to their delegators? This article covers how EffectStream makes this possible.
entry.content:
WRITE: Cardano's stake pool ecosystem has millions of ADA delegated across hundreds of pools, but delegation has been a one-way relationship — delegators earn rewards, and that's it. What if games and apps could react to where you delegate? What if stake pool operators could offer in-game benefits to their delegators? This article covers how EffectStream makes this possible.

Indexing Stake Delegations​

The first step is knowing who delegates to which pool. We built a Carp indexer task that tracks stake delegations across the Cardano network, with an OpenAPI definition so any application can query delegation status.

ADD: Link to OpenAPI spec

WRITE: What makes indexing delegation different from indexing transactions — delegation is a ledger state change, not a transaction in the traditional sense. The indexer needs to track the current state of every stake address, not just process individual events.

Connecting Delegation Data to EffectStream​

We extended EffectStream's funnel system to ingest delegation data as first-class events. When a user changes their delegation, EffectStream apps receive this as an event they can react to — just like they react to smart contract events or token transfers.

  • Implementation PR
  • Documentation

WRITE: Technical deep-dive on ledger state vs. on-chain actions — delegation changes are ledger state transitions, not traditional transactions. This creates interesting challenges: how do you detect a change that isn't an explicit event? How do you handle epoch boundaries? (Get Enzo's input on the nuances)

ADD VIDEO HERE explaining the integration architecture

NOTE: Docs need migration — this primitive is not yet implemented in v2

Reacting to Delegation Changes in Game Logic​

With delegation data flowing into the state machine, developers can build game logic that reacts to where players delegate:

  • Unlock content: "This area is accessible only to delegators of Pool X"
  • Distribute rewards: "All delegators of Pool Y receive a bonus item at epoch boundary"
  • Dynamic difficulty: Adjust game parameters based on which pools your players support

IMPLEMENT: Need to build an example state machine demonstrating these patterns

BLOCKED: Cardano localhost testing is difficult — no Cardano wallet currently supports localhost connections (unlike MetaMask for EVM). This makes it hard to create a live demo, though the architecture works in production.

NOTE: Several Cardano primitives still need migration to v2: Pool Delegation, Delayed State, Transfer, Mint/Burn, Projected NFT

WRITE: Even without a live demo, describe the architecture with a diagram: delegation event → Carp indexer → funnel → state machine → game state update. Show what a handler function looks like in code.

Batcher Pool Verification​

We also added pool-aware batching. The batcher can check which pool an address delegates to before processing a transaction — enabling scenarios like free batching for users delegating to partner pools.

  • Implementation PR
  • Configuration: BATCHER_CARDANO_ENABLED_POOLS environment variable

WRITE: Expand on the use case — a stake pool operator partners with a game. Their delegators get free transaction batching (the SPO covers the cost). This creates a new incentive for delegation beyond staking rewards: "delegate to our pool and play for free."

Conclusion — Vision for SPO-Powered Gameplay​

WRITE: The bigger picture — stake pool delegation becomes a game mechanic. SPOs offer in-game benefits, players choose pools based on gameplay advantages, and a new economic layer emerges on top of Cardano's existing delegation system. This transforms delegation from a passive yield-earning activity into an active strategic decision.

DECISION: Need direction from Nico — what's the current roadmap? Midnight Cities path or alternative?

ADD VIDEO HERE if possible — even a conceptual demo or architecture walkthrough

entry.title: Streamlining User Experience: AI, Delegated Wallets, and Social Login
entry.summary: WRITE AI-powered gameplay, delegated signing, social login, and biometrics.
entry.content:
WRITE: Blockchain apps suffer from UX friction — constant wallet pop-ups, complex key management, no familiar login flows. This article covers a suite of integrations that make blockchain apps feel like regular web apps: AI-powered gameplay, delegated signing, social login, and biometrics.

Browser AI with Shinkai Visor​

We released the [Shinkai Visor](ADD LINK) Chrome extension, which connects LLMs directly to the browser context. Users can bring their own AI models into applications — the extension provides a bridge between on-chain game state and AI inference.

INVESTIGATE: Is Shinkai Visor still available? Is the API still running?

WRITE: Explain the value prop — AI agents that can see and interact with on-chain game state. What can a user do with this that they couldn't do before?

AI-Powered Game Template​

To demonstrate what's possible when AI meets on-chain games, we built a game template that integrates Shinkai Visor for AI-driven game mechanics.

  • Taiko game (live)
  • Template code

INVESTIGATE: Does the Taiko game need redeployment? Is the Shinkai API still up?

DECISION: Open-source Token Heist (https://github.com/PaimaStudios/taiko-shinkai-game)?

WRITE: How does the game leverage the LLM? What decisions does the AI make? What's the player experience?

ADD VIDEO HERE showing the AI integration in gameplay

Device-Specific Key Pairs​

One of the biggest UX problems in blockchain apps is wallet pop-ups. Every action that touches the chain requires the user to approve a signature. For games where you make dozens of moves per session, this is unacceptable.

Our solution: delegated wallet signing. Non-financial transactions use a local device key pair — no wallet pop-ups for routine actions. Only financial transactions (transfers, minting) require explicit wallet approval.

  • Documentation

WRITE: Explain the delegation model in detail — what gets delegated, what doesn't, and why this is safe. How is the device key pair generated and stored?

INVESTIGATE: Can we show the Tarochi integration? Note: Tarochi is currently down

ADD VIDEO HERE demonstrating the delegation flow — show a user approving delegation once, then interacting freely

Social Login​

For users who don't have a crypto wallet at all, we integrated social login via the thirdweb SDK. This gives applications access to multiple authentication methods under a single SDK:

  • In-memory wallets (free tier)
  • Social login (email, Google, etc.)
  • Multiple wallet providers

Users sign in with their email or social account once, then interact with the blockchain seamlessly — no wallet extension required.

  • Documentation

WRITE: Why we picked thirdweb — multiple wallet types under one SDK, free in-memory option, competitive paid social login tier. Compare briefly to alternatives.

BUILD DEMO: Need a working demo showing social login → game interaction

ADD VIDEO HERE

Biometric Authentication​

INVESTIGATE: What work by Lucas exists? Is there an implementation?

The concept: use FaceID on iOS or Android's standard biometric authentication to sign blockchain transactions. Biometrics become another key source for the delegated key pair — combining the security of biometric verification with the convenience of automatic signing.

DECISION: Can we demonstrate this? What's the current state of the implementation?

WRITE: How biometrics fit into the delegation model — another key source for the delegated key pair. What's the security model?

ADD VIDEO HERE if available

Conclusion​

WRITE: Together, these features remove the biggest UX barriers to blockchain adoption. A new user can sign in with their email (social login), approve delegation once (device key pairs), and then play a game with AI opponents (Shinkai Visor) — all without ever seeing a wallet pop-up or understanding what a private key is.

ADD VIDEO HERE: Close-out showing the full flow from social login to gameplay
